Is JavaScript Sync or Async? The Most Misunderstood Thing About the Language

All over the internet you'll find people saying JavaScript is an asynchronous language. But after poking at how it actually runs, I realized this is a pretty big misunderstanding. So in this post let's talk about whether JavaScript is really async or sync.

JavaScript started out mainly as a language for running in the browser. A big part of why it got so popular is that it doesn't eat up server resources. Early on it only ran in browsers — but then Node.js showed up, and that changed the language entirely.
The first thing you'll notice if you Google around is people calling JavaScript a
Let's start with single-threaded. Basically, single-threaded means it can only do one thing at a time. That part's easy enough to grasp.

So what about asynchronous? First, synchronous basically means "one step at a time" — it runs line by line, in order. Asynchronous means it doesn't necessarily go line by line; it might skip ahead. So think about this example:
This is the classic example a lot of articles use to argue that JavaScript is asynchronous, because the output shows
. But does that actually mean JavaScript is async? Nope. Why?
When JavaScript runs, it didn't ignore the
line. What it did was hand
off to one of JavaScript's features — the so-called event queue.
in one of Kacper's posts (in Mandarin).
JavaScript runs following the synchronous model. When it hits something that needs to be
— it tosses that into the event queue and just moves on to the next line.
You can actually piece together from a few sources that JavaScript is synchronous. For example, in a post I wrote earlier —
[JS Weird World] No.6 Code Execution (article in Mandarin)
— I mentioned that "only after all the variables are created does it start executing line by line and assigning values."
There's also a thread on Stack Overflow,
, where plenty of people say the same thing:
JavaScript is always synchronous and single-threaded.
JavaScript is single threaded and has a synchronous execution model.
JavaScript is single-threaded, and all the time you work on a normal synchronous code-flow execution.
So why do so many people mistakenly think JavaScript is async? The main culprit is that event queue feature.
There's also a Medium article making the same point that JavaScript is synchronous →
Is JavaScript Synchronous or Asynchronous? What the Hell is a Promise?
That Medium post has a couple of diagrams I really like. First, JavaScript runs through the code once:
JavaScript runs through the code once first
Then it drops the stuff that needs to happen asynchronously into the event queue:
JavaScript drops async events into the event queue
You can see that JavaScript first runs through all the code once, and pushes the functions that need to be handled asynchronously into the event queue.
